patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v5 0/7] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
Re: [RFC PATCH 1/4] cxl/extent: Promote cxlr_dax->region_extent to an xarray

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 0/4] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 0/4] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/4] mm: track DONTCACHE dirty pages per bdi_writeback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new counter, WB_DONTCACHE_DIRTY, to track the number of dirty pages that have been dirtied via RWF_DONTCACHE writes (i.e., pages with the dropbehind flag set). The counter is incremented when a folio is dirtied and decremented when it's cleaned or cleared. This allows the writeback flusher to expedite writeback for IOCB_DONTCACHE writes without flushing the entire BDI's dirty pages. The patch modifies several kernel functions, including folio_account_dirtied(), folio_clear_dirty_for_io(), and __filemap_get_folio_mpol().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/4] mm: kick writeback flusher for IOCB_DONTCACHE with targeted dirty tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's writeback behavior to improve performance in the IOCB_DONTCACHE path. The original code submitted writeback requests inline, causing significant latency and contention. This patch introduces a background flusher that writes back dirty pages in the background, reducing the impact on the writer's hot path. To limit unnecessary flushing, it uses a dedicated bit and counter to target only the necessary dirty pages. The result is improved throughput and reduced tail latency for sequential write workloads.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 3/4] testing: add nfsd-io-bench NFS server benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a benchmark suite to test NFSD I/O mode performance using fio against an NFS server on localhost. The suite includes various workloads such as sequential and random read/write, multi-writer, noisy-neighbor, and latency-sensitive reader tests. It also includes scripts for setting up the NFS export, running the benchmarks, and parsing the results. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive testing framework for evaluating NFSD I/O mode performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] testing: add dontcache-bench local filesystem benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite called 'dontcache-bench' to test the IOCB_DONTCACHE flag on local filesystems using fio's io_uring engine. The suite includes various tests, such as competing buffered vs. dontcache writers and a benchmark mirroring Jens Axboe's original RWF_UNCACHED write test. The patch also includes job files for fio, scripts to run the benchmarks and parse results, and makes changes to several files in the kernel source tree.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve write performance by implementing a scheme that kicks the flusher thread after a DONTCACHE write, targeting an amount of writeback proportional to the amount written.

review_comments.author: Jens Axboe
review_comments.summary: Approved the approach of kicking the flusher thread after a DONTCACHE write, considering it a better way to initiate writeback.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/1/26 3:49 AM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> The IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path in generic_write_sync() calls
> filemap_flush_range() on every write, submitting writeback inline in
> the writer's context.  Perf lock contention profiling shows the
> performance problem is not lock contention but the writeback submission
> work itself \u2014 walking the page tree and submitting I/O blocks the writer
> for milliseconds, inflating p99.9 latency from 23ms (buffered) to 93ms
> (dontcache).
> 
> Replace the inline filemap_flush_range() call with a flusher kick that
> drains dirty pages in the background.  This moves writeback submission
> completely off the writer's hot path.
> 
> To avoid flushing unrelated buffered dirty data, add a dedicated
> WB_start_dontcache bit and wb_check_start_dontcache() handler that uses
> the per-wb WB_DONTCACHE_DIRTY counter to determine how many pages to
> write back.  The flusher writes back that many pages from the oldest dirty
> inodes (not restricted to dontcache-specific inodes). This helps
> preserve I/O batching while limiting the scope of expedited writeback.
> 
> Like WB_start_all, the WB_start_dontcache bit coalesces multiple
> DONTCACHE writes into a single flusher wakeup without per-write
> allocations.
> 
> Also add WB_REASON_DONTCACHE as a new writeback reason for tracing
> visibility, and target the correct cgroup writeback domain via
> unlocked_inode_to_wb_begin().
> 
> dontcache-bench results (same host, T6F_SKL_1920GBF, 251 GiB RAM,
> xfs on NVMe, fio io_uring):
> 
> Buffered and direct I/O paths are unaffected by this patchset. All
> improvements are confined to the dontcache path:
> 
> Single-stream throughput (MB/s):
>                         Before    After    Change
>   seq-write/dontcache      298      897    +201%
>   rand-write/dontcache     131      236     +80%
> 
> Tail latency improvements (seq-write/dontcache):
>   p99:    135,266 us  ->  23,986 us   (-82%)
>   p99.9: 8,925,479 us ->  28,443 us   (-99.7%)
> 
> Multi-writer (4 jobs, sequential write):
>                                 Before    After    Change
>   dontcache aggregate (MB/s)     2,529    4,532     +79%
>   dontcache p99 (us)             8,553    1,002     -88%
>   dontcache p99.9 (us)         109,314    1,057     -99%
> 
>   Dontcache multi-writer throughput now matches buffered (4,532 vs
>   4,616 MB/s).
> 
> 32-file write (Axboe test):
>                                 Before    After    Change
>   dontcache aggregate (MB/s)     1,548    3,499    +126%
>   dontcache p99 (us)            10,170      602     -94%
>   Peak dirty pages (MB)          1,837      213     -88%
> 
>   Dontcache now reaches 81% of buffered throughput (was 35%).
> 
> Competing writers (dontcache vs buffered, separate files):
>                                 Before    After
>   buffered writer                  868      433 MB/s
>   dontcache writer                 415      433 MB/s
>   Aggregate                      1,284      866 MB/s
> 
>   Previously the buffered writer starved the dontcache writer 2:1.
>   With per-bdi_writeback tracking, both writers now receive equal
>   bandwidth. The aggregate matches the buffered-vs-buffered baseline
>   (863 MB/s), indicating fair sharing regardless of I/O mode.
> 
>   The dontcache writer's p99.9 latency collapsed from 119 ms to
>   33 ms (-73%), eliminating the severe periodic stalls seen in the
>   baseline. Both writers now share identical latency profiles,
>   matching the buffered-vs-buffered pattern.
> 
> The per-bdi_writeback dirty tracking dramatically reduces peak dirty
> pages in dontcache workloads, with the 32-file test dropping from
> 1.8 GB to 213 MB. Dontcache sequential write throughput triples and
> multi-writer throughput reaches parity with buffered I/O, with tail
> latencies collapsing by 1-2 orders of magnitude.

I like this, this is the better way to kick off the writeback.

Reviewed-by: Jens Axboe <axboe@kernel.dk>

-- 
Jens Axboe

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [BUG] [powerpc] [next-20260216/17] nfsd: use-after-free in cache_check_rcu() triggered by sosreport on ppc64le
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch does not actually introduce any changes, but rather a response to a bug report. The developer suggests that the issue may be related to a merge commit on x86 drivers and recommends running tests with KASAN enabled to gather more information about the object being freed.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the reported bug was previously addressed in v7.0, but there are multiple instances of the same use-after-free (UAF) class and a new patch series is being worked on to address these issues.

reviewer noted that they have an internal bug reporting the same stack trace on ARM64, and suggested using this platform to confirm the issues are fully addressed before merging new fixes
review_comments.raw_body:
You didn’t miss it. We responded to that report in February with
a fix that you and Neil both reviewed and it is in v7.0 now.

That fix addressed one instance of this problem; there are actually
several more, all in the same UAF class. I have a patch series nearly
complete to address these issues.

The issue Misbah reports here is not the same as the February issue,
though it is very similar.

---

We have an internal bug reporting the same stack trace on ARM64. Now
that we have both a reproducer and a platform that is easily accessible
it will be straightforward to confirm these issues are fully addressed
before we merge the new fixes.


-- 
Chuck Lever

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 4/4] fuse: propagate default and file acls on creation
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses issues in the fuse file system's handling of POSIX ACLs during file creation. It includes suggestions for improving code clarity, consistency, and correctness. The changes include renaming a function, reordering variable declarations, removing unnecessary null initialization, adding a FUSE_RELEASE to prevent memory leaks, and simplifying ACL logic by moving it to caller functions.

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that the patch will leak an allocated fuse_file and requested a FUSE_RELEASE be sent to the server so it can release state.

Reviewer Darrick Wong expressed frustration with static checkers complaining about unnecessary variable initialization and suggested that the code should focus on getting the logic right rather than optimizing for these tools.

Reviewer noted that the patch needs to perform cleanups similar to the failure case for fuse_iget, specifically moving the fuse_init_acls call up in the code to ensure proper resource release.

Reviewer noted that create_new_entry() should not consume acl/default_acl, and suggested modifying the function to achieve this

Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested moving a specific code hunk to be adjacent to the previous similar code, for consistency and clarity.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that skipping posix_acl_create() call in symlink case encodes implementation detail in fuse, potentially causing logic bomb if posix_acl_create() is modified to handle symlinks differently
